effect_text: The second time you discard a card each turn, your next card this turn costs 2● less.
image_alt_text: Golden fruit-like charm with a mesh-patterned orb and smooth teardrop front.
justification: The acorn reads like a stored seed that only pays off after a second discard, when the next card finally gets the discount.

effect_text: When you play your first character in a turn, the next character you play this turn costs 2● less.
image_alt_text: Blue-gray square panel with bright red-orange branching nodes and lines.
justification: The branching panel feels like one action unlocking the next, which matches the first character setting up a cheaper second one.

effect_text: The first character you play each turn costs 2● less if an ally left play this turn.
image_alt_text: Red spotted mushroom with white flecks and pale stem.
justification: A mushroom after something leaves play fits the idea of new growth getting cheaper once an ally has died or been tucked away.

effect_text: The first character you draw each turn costs 2● less until end of turn.
image_alt_text: Shimmering lime-green ribbon or liquid fabric twisting in soft waves.
justification: The flowing algae ribbon suggests the first draw slipping forward into the rest of the turn, making the next character lighter to play.

effect_text: The first character you play each turn costs 0●. Its spark becomes 1.
image_alt_text: Shiny red apple with green segmented stem and curled leaf.
justification: The apple is the simplest starter object here, so it suits a first character that comes down for free and leaves behind a little spark.

effect_text: The second card you draw each turn costs 2● less.
image_alt_text: Bright green faceted gemstone pendant in a gold setting.
justification: The bright gem amulet feels like a small reserve of value, which is a natural fit for discounting the second card you draw.

effect_text: Your second event each turn costs 1● less.
image_alt_text: Gold-rimmed circular lens filled with purple and magenta clouds.
justification: The cloudy lens looks like a second look through haze, which is close enough to a second event costing less.

effect_text: The first card you play each turn after you discard a card costs 1● less.
image_alt_text: Gray wolf mask medallion with orange gem and dark beads.
justification: The wolf mask suggests a hunt that only starts after something is shed, so the first card after a discard getting cheaper feels right.

effect_text: When you return an ally to hand or banish an ally, the next character you play this turn costs 1● less.
image_alt_text: Beaded gold necklace with hanging claws, tusks, and colored charms.
justification: The clawed necklace implies pulling something back into reach, which lines up with returning or banishing an ally and then easing the next character into play.

effect_text: When you return an ally to hand, it costs 1● less this turn.
image_alt_text: Small hanging talisman with beige face, orange beads, and metal tools.
justification: This little talisman already looks like a tool for handling things in hand, so reducing the cost to return an ally is a clean fit.

effect_text: The first ↯fast card you play during the opponent's turn costs 1● less.
image_alt_text: Gold triangular eye amulet with horns and carved sunburst details.
justification: The eye charm reads like something that watches for the enemy turn, so the first fast card during that turn getting cheaper makes sense.

effect_text: Characters with ▸ Materialized cost you 1● less.
image_alt_text: Skull pendant with dangling chains, beige bone and dark eye sockets.
justification: The skull pendant is a strong stand-in for materialized bodies, and the cost reduction feels like dead weight being stripped away.

effect_text: The first character you materialize each turn has Unbound.
image_alt_text: Blue metal medallion with purple gem and small orb-like charm.
justification: The blue medallion feels airy and untethered, which matches the first materialized character each turn having Unbound.

effect_text: Once per turn, when you abandon an ally, trigger its ▸ Materialized: ability.
image_alt_text: Brown leather satchel with gold buckle, stitching, and flap closure.
justification: A travel satchel is where you stow something before bringing it back out, so it fits abandoning an ally to trigger its effect.

effect_text: Once per turn, when you abandon an ally, materialize a 1-spark copy of it.
image_alt_text: Gray cloth pouch tied at the neck, glowing with cyan light.
justification: The glowing pouch suggests a stored copy or duplicate, which is close enough to abandoning an ally and materializing a 1-spark version.

effect_text: When you banish an ally and then materialize it, its Materialized ability gains Echo
image_alt_text: deep red leather pouch with gold cord, blue beads, and stitched flap
justification: The charm-filled pouch feels like a container for repeated tricks, so banishing and rematerializing an ally gaining Echo works well.

effect_text: Once per turn, when you banish and materialize an ally, draw a card.
image_alt_text: bluish-gray bat wing with dark shadows and a sleek curved membrane
justification: Bat wings come from the dark and carry an obvious echo of the void, which makes the echoed materialization ability feel natural.

effect_text: When you play a character from your void, its ▸ Materialized: ability gains Echo
image_alt_text: ivory and pink bell with red ribbon tied around the top
justification: A bell is literally about triggering another ring after the first one, so it is a good stand-in for an ability that repeats and grows spark.

effect_text: At end of turn, if you materialized 2 allies, trigger the last one's ▸ Materialized ability.
image_alt_text: green-and-black berry-like orb surrounded by yellow spotted petals
justification: Belladonna is a poisonous night bloom, and that final burst of effect after two allies materialize has the same last-flash energy.

effect_text: When a ▸ Materialized: ability of an ally triggers, that ally gains +1 base spark.
image_alt_text: multiple fantasy item icons on dark background, dominated by varied reds, golds, blues, and greens
justification: The clustered berries suggest many small yields building into one stronger harvest, which matches a trigger that adds base spark.

effect_text: Your characters with energy cost 5 or greater have Unbound.
image_alt_text: bright green beetle with yellow highlights and glossy segmented shell
justification: The beetle shell reads as armored and self-contained, which is a decent image for high-cost characters that stay Unbound.

effect_text: If you abandoned a character this turn, characters you materialize have unbound.
image_alt_text: brown and orange pile of rounded wooden nuts or seeds
justification: A bezoar is a strange protective charm, so it can plausibly loosen restraints on the characters you materialize after an abandon.

effect_text: Whenever you add a Warrior card to your deck, increase its base spark by 1.
image_alt_text: amber acorn with tan cap and warm glowing highlights
justification: An acorn is the right symbol for a Warrior seed that grows stronger whenever you add one to the deck.

effect_text: While you control 3 or more Warriors, events you play gain Echo.
image_alt_text: gray-blue rose blossom with pale green stem and leaves
justification: The black rose feels like a bloom that keeps repeating its own pattern, which works for events echoing while you have enough Warriors.

effect_text: When you play your second character each turn, materialize a Warrior figment.
image_alt_text: clear blue jar holding a black spider with red markings
justification: A spider in a jar evokes extra bodies and a second thread of motion, so it suits materializing a Warrior figment on your second character play.

effect_text: ▸ Judgment: If the top card of your deck is a Warrior, materialize it instead of drawing a card this turn.
image_alt_text: pale cyan feather with soft translucent barbs on a white background
justification: The feather looks like a card lifted up into motion, so turning a revealed Warrior into a materialized one instead of drawing is easy to read into.

effect_text: Your Warriors have Preeminence.
image_alt_text: tan bone pipe or whistle with rounded joints and hollow end
justification: The bone horn has a clear rallying-call feel, which fits Warriors carrying Preeminence as the front line.

effect_text: Your events cost 1● less while you control 2+ Warriors.
image_alt_text: Tan drum with carved skull lid, brown body, and gold tension cords.
justification: A spellbook naturally lowers the cost of the magic it contains, so the discount for events while you have enough Warriors lands cleanly.

effect_text: Judgment: If you control 3 or more Warriors, draw a card.
image_alt_text: ivory curved claw or tusk with brown spots
justification: The paw-marked codex hints at a pack in formation, which makes drawing a card once you control enough Warriors feel earned.

effect_text: Supported Warriors have +1 spark and Preeminence.
image_alt_text: curved black staff with copper wrappings, feathers, and dark hanging charms
justification: The jellyfish book looks like something sustained by the whole tide, which is a decent image for supported Warriors getting extra spark and stature.

effect_text: Your Warriors cost 1● less.
image_alt_text: curved silver blade with black grip, tan guard, and cyan glowing background
justification: The lightning book is the most direct cost-cutting image in the set, so it fits Warriors becoming cheaper to deploy.

effect_text: Warriors in your void have Reclaim equal to their cost.
image_alt_text: sleek black-and-silver droplet of liquid or polished metal
justification: The eye grimoire feels like it keeps watch over what has fallen away, which suits Warriors in the void coming back with Reclaim.

effect_text: Allied Figments count as Warriors.
image_alt_text: cream theatrical mask with black eye makeup and dark hair backing
justification: The skull tome reads like a ledger of the dead, making it easy to accept Figments counting as full Warriors.

effect_text: ▸ Judgment: Each allied Figment gains +1 spark.
image_alt_text: Dark gray stone with bright cyan rune-like symbol inset.
justification: The serpent book has a living, invested feel, which matches allied Figments each gaining spark when Judgment lands.

effect_text: ▸ Judgment: If you have 4 or more allied Figments, gain 2●.
image_alt_text: Reddish-orange sack spilling pale rune-marked stones or shells.
justification: The leaf ledger suggests a small stockpile of growth, so gaining energy when enough Figments are assembled feels plausible.

effect_text: When you discard a card, materialize a Radiant Figment.
image_alt_text: large faceted purple crystal on a brown pedestal
justification: The gem codex radiates from the cover, which makes discarding a card and calling up a Radiant Figment feel like a spark from the pages.

effect_text: Judgment: A random card in your hand costs 1● this turn.
image_alt_text: dark gray mortar holding bright yellow-green potion with a gold pestle
justification: Grinding in a mortar is a good visual for narrowing a hand down, so a random card getting cheaper for the turn fits the bowl well.

effect_text: Judgment: Lose 1●. Draw a card.
image_alt_text: brown broom with red band and glowing yellow-green bristles
justification: A broom sweeps something away and leaves room for what comes next, which is close enough to losing energy and drawing a card.

effect_text: Judgment: Put the top 2 cards of your deck into your void.
image_alt_text: Two turquoise ice crystals with white veins and glossy rounded facets.
justification: Ice naturally seals things under a cold layer, so putting the top two cards into the void feels like freezing them out of play.

effect_text: Judgment: If two or more of your characters are deployed, draw a card, then discard a card.
image_alt_text: teal tarot card with gold crescent moon and star symbols
justification: A tarot card naturally suggests a draw-and-discard shuffle, which fits the way extra deployed characters make the turn cycle faster and messier.

effect_text: Judgment: Gain 1●. When drafting cards, see 1 fewer choices.
image_alt_text: Pale ivory circular shells or coins, layered and glossy, on warm tan background.
justification: The layered shell-like tokens look like a payment laid out in advance, so gaining a little energy while surrendering one draft option feels like spending from a limited purse.

effect_text: Judgment: Add a random fast card from the card pool to your hand.
image_alt_text: Rainbow twisted horn or horn-shaped spiral, fading from green to orange.
justification: A bright horn feels like the sort of charm that sounds an instant response, which fits pulling a random fast card straight into hand.

effect_text: Your leftmost ally's ▸ Judgment: ability triggers an additional time.
image_alt_text: Wooden hourglass with pale sand, tilted in a warm brown frame.
justification: An hourglass suggests the same moment being stretched or repeated, so the leftmost ally firing its Judgment ability again is an easy fit.

effect_text: A random Judgment ability of an ally each turn gains Echo.
image_alt_text: red and black butterfly with white-spotted wing edges
justification: Wings already imply mirrored repetition, so a random judgment ability echoing on one ally feels like the same pattern rippling outward.

effect_text: ▸ Judgment: Play a copy of the last event you played last turn for 0●.
image_alt_text: Dark red woven orb with olive beads, wrapped in brown cords and gray tassels.
justification: A dreamcatcher literally traps an old dream and releases it again, which is close enough to replaying the last event you used.

effect_text: ▸ Judgment: Return a random event you played last turn from your void to your hand.
image_alt_text: brown hooded stone or shell with swirling cream streaks and a circular dark opening
justification: The hooded idol looks like something you petition for one lost favor, which makes calling a random event back from the void feel plausible.

effect_text: Judgment: Return a random Survivor from your void to your hand.
image_alt_text: Beige rope doll with flowing strands, tied in loose knots, on orange-brown background.
justification: The tied doll looks like something you can tug back by its strings, which suits pulling a Survivor out of the void and back into hand.

effect_text: ▸ Judgment: You may abandon a character to draw a card.
image_alt_text: blue-gray rat with red eyes on a brown branch
justification: The image is already a discarded body, so sacrificing a character to draw a card has the right grim trade attached to it.

effect_text: When you copy an event, copy it an additional time.
image_alt_text: green book with a turquoise swirling gem set in the cover
justification: Faceted crystal naturally suggests refraction and duplication, which makes an extra copy on top of a copied event feel plausible.

effect_text: Copy the first event you play each turn.
image_alt_text: black cat figurine with glowing amber eyes and curled tail
justification: The cat figurine carries the usual nine-life superstition, which is an easy way to read the first event of the turn coming back again.

effect_text: Your events that cost 2● or less are copied.
image_alt_text: gray wooden book with a bright orange lightning bolt across the cover
justification: A claw leaves more than one scratch, so copying cheap events feels like the same sharp motion landing twice instead of once.

effect_text: With no allies in play, your events gain Echo.
image_alt_text: pale green clover leaf with white ringed center pattern
justification: Clover is the obvious luck charm, and events gaining Echo when you have no allies matches that solitary good-fortune vibe.

effect_text: Once per turn, when you copy an event, gain 1●.
image_alt_text: orange glowing ember-like disc surrounded by soft pink aura
justification: A button is something you press to trigger a response, which fits getting a small burst of energy when you copy an event.

effect_text: When you play an event, each focused ally gains +1 spark.
image_alt_text: black raven with glossy feathers and a pale blue eye
justification: Crows are loud heralds, so having an event kindle allied spark feels like the bird calling the rest of the flock to attention.

effect_text: Your events have reclaim equal to their cost.
image_alt_text: black cauldron filled with bubbling neon green liquid
justification: A cauldron is a vessel that keeps ingredients cycling, which matches reclaim behaving like a cost that gets brewed back into the event.

effect_text: The first event you play from your void each turn costs 0●.
image_alt_text: silver dagger with a skull hilt and dark handle
justification: A knife cuts things down to size, which is a decent visual for making the first void-played event cost nothing.

effect_text: The first event you reclaim each turn gains Echo.
image_alt_text: tan book with a glowing red eye and cracked mystical cover
justification: Dark liquid in a vial feels like stored power that can rebound, which suits the first reclaimed event also gaining Echo.

effect_text: Characters with cost 5● or more in your void have Reclaim 3●.
image_alt_text: light green plant sprouting many eye-like nodules from a cracked pot
justification: The eye-like nodules make it feel watchful and stubborn, which is a reasonable image for a big void card hanging around with extra reclaim.

effect_text: You may play cards with the Reclaim ability from your void for Reclaim 0.
image_alt_text: black and gray book with skull emblem and bone corner guards
justification: A drum sets a beat, so pulling a card from the void and drawing again feels like the next strike in the rhythm.

effect_text: When you play a card from your void, draw a card.
image_alt_text: Glowing red amber egg with fiery yellow core and dark crack lines.
justification: A dragon egg is latent power waiting to hatch, which fits a second-event card coming back with reclaim attached.

effect_text: When you play your second event each turn, it gains Reclaim 0●.
image_alt_text: Yellow-green spotted egg with large dark blotches and smooth rounded surface.
justification: This egg is a sealed promise, so the first card you bury into the void keeping its own reclaim feels like stored potential.

effect_text: You may play fast events for 1●.
image_alt_text: Red-orange faceted gem or crystal with rough, glowing, rounded shape.
justification: Powder is the cleanest image for a quick burst of magic, so making fast events cheap reads like tossing a pinch into the spell.

effect_text: The first time each turn you abandon an ally or discard a character, that card gains Reclaim equal to its cost.
image_alt_text: Blue glass vial containing black smoky liquid, capped with a brown stopper.
justification: A vial of essence feels like preserved fuel, which is close enough to a discarded ally or character keeping its reclaim value.

effect_text: The first card you put into your void from your deck each turn gains reclaim equal to its cost.
image_alt_text: Green fly with translucent wings, brown legs, and copper eyes.
justification: Flies swarm the forgotten and the dead, so drawing a card off a crowded void pile has the right buzz of decay and motion.

effect_text: When you play an event, kindle 1.
image_alt_text: cream candle with golden flame and melted wax drips
justification: A candle is the obvious spark source, so kindle 1 is basically the object doing what it does by existing.

effect_text: When a card enters your void from your deck, kindle 1.
image_alt_text: Red anatomical heart with glossy veins and rounded chambers.
justification: A heart is the emblem of living spark, so feeding discarded cards into it and getting kindle from the deck feels natural.

effect_text: When you abandon an ally, kindle X where X is that ally's spark.
image_alt_text: Single black feather with layered barbs and a thin dark shaft.
justification: Feathers fall off a living bird, so abandoning an ally and turning its spark into kindle reads like shedding something alive.

effect_text: When you abandon a character, kindle 1.
image_alt_text: White-gold feather glowing softly against a vivid yellow background.
justification: A bright feather suggests a cleaner loss, which makes a simple one-point kindle when a character goes away feel easy to read.

effect_text: When you kindle, double the amount of spark gained
image_alt_text: Pale gray feather with curved quill and soft layered barbs.
justification: A bundle of feathers feels like concentrated lift, so doubling kindle gains reads like piling more flight onto the same flame.

effect_text: While you have 8 or more cards in your void, draw a card when you play an event.
image_alt_text: Bright golden eye with black slit pupil and glowing amber iris.
justification: An eye is the clearest foresee symbol, and looking three cards ahead matches the idea of seeing the battle before it happens.

effect_text: When you play your first event each turn, Foresee 1.
image_alt_text: White garlic bulb with creamy cloves and faint beige shading.
justification: Garlic wards off bad outcomes, so the first-event foresee trigger feels like a protective herb giving you a safe peek ahead.

effect_text: Once per turn, when you foresee, draw a card.
image_alt_text: Golden ginger root with sliced yellow pieces and warm brown shadows.
justification: A hot root wakes you up, which makes it easy to read as the extra draw that follows a moment of foresight.

effect_text: At the start of each battle, Foresee 3. Cards you leave on top this way cost 1● less.
image_alt_text: Bundled lock of brown hair tied at one end.
justification: Hair is something you comb into place, so arranging the top of the deck at battle start matches the image's strand-like order.

effect_text: When you play your second event in a turn, draw a card.
image_alt_text: Dark brown witch hat with a black band and silver buckle.
justification: A witch hat is what you wear when the second trick comes out, so drawing on the second event feels like the right moment for it.

effect_text: Once per turn, when you discard a card, gain 1●.
image_alt_text: Three amber gem shards set in pale cream honeycomb frames.
justification: Honeycomb stores sweetness in cells, so a discard paying back a small energy gain reads like a little harvest from the spent piece.

effect_text: If you have discarded a card this turn, the first card you play from your void costs 1 less.
image_alt_text: green spiky cactus with small offshoots and pale thorns
justification: A cactus only looks easy to touch after you have already dealt with the spikes, which is close enough to the first void play becoming cheaper after a discard.

effect_text: Your events cost 1● less for each card you discarded this turn.
image_alt_text: two brown star anise blossoms on warm amber background
justification: The spice pods suggest a turn getting more concentrated the longer you work it, so events costing less for each discard feels like the flavor intensifying.

effect_text: Once per turn, when you discard a card, draw a card.
image_alt_text: olive green leafy branch bound with yellow twine
justification: A bundle of leaves is what falls and gets gathered together, which makes the discard-then-draw loop feel like a natural cycle of shedding and regrowth.

effect_text: The first time each turn you play a Spirit Animal, draw a card.
image_alt_text: yellow-gold fish with orange scales and bright fins
justification: A spirit animal is still an animal, and a fish totem reads like a charm you call on when the turn begins. Catching one should loosen the next card from the deck.

effect_text: Allied characters with Judgment abilities have +1 spark.
image_alt_text: blue-gray book featuring a mint-green jellyfish over ocean waves
justification: A mask fits judgment literally: it gives the ally a role to speak from. In a courtroom sort of way, that extra authority becomes spark.

effect_text: Your deployed characters with two supporting characters have +2 spark.
image_alt_text: Brown ringed bracelet with black beads and tan claw-like hanging charms.
justification: Two supporting characters feel like matched charms on the same chain. The necklace image makes the pair read as a shared anchor that buffs the whole deployed line.

effect_text: Allied Survivors have +2 spark.
image_alt_text: Pink speckled mushroom cap with two hollow dark openings.
justification: A mushroom survives by coming back after pressure, which is close enough to a Survivor tribe. The image sells hardiness more than elegance, which is the point.

effect_text: Your deployed characters that share a subtype with another deployed character you control have +2 spark.
image_alt_text: Golden autumn leaf with brown veins and glowing amber edges.
justification: Leaves on the same branch are the same sort of thing, just not quite the same leaf. That is a decent squint-match for a subtype bonus.

effect_text: Your characters with cost 5● or more have +2 spark.
image_alt_text: Iridescent oval gem shimmering orange, yellow, green, and blue.
justification: Opals are precious enough to feel like the expensive end of the roster. Bigger, costlier characters getting more spark reads like the prize stone lighting them up.

effect_text: While you control the deployed character with the highest spark, your deployed characters have +1 spark.
image_alt_text: glossy pink and purple crystal sphere on a brown pedestal
justification: The highest-spark character becomes the focal point, and the orb makes that feel literal. Once one piece is ahead, the whole board seems to orbit it.

effect_text: Your deployed characters have +1 spark while your reserve is empty.
image_alt_text: Orange-yellow eggs nestled in a woven brown nest.
justification: An empty reserve feels like an emptied nest: everything has already been committed to the field. That makes the deployed team feel more settled and stronger.

effect_text: Supported allies have +1 spark and Dread 1.
image_alt_text: bright green slime blob covered in multiple blinking eyeballs
justification: A many-eyed slime can support allies while still being unsettling. The dread rider feels like a creepy aura that clings to anything standing near it.

effect_text: The first character you play each turn materializes with +1 spark.
image_alt_text: silver metal key with round ornate bow and long cylindrical shaft
justification: The first character is the one that unlocks the turn, so a key is a natural stand-in. It starts the board state and gets a little extra force.

effect_text: Characters you materialize gain +1 spark for each other character materialized this turn.
image_alt_text: cream-white segmented grub with red head, orange legs, and black mandibles
justification: Larvae get stronger by existing in numbers, which is basically what this effect does. Each extra materialized ally makes the next one feel more alive.

effect_text: Allied characters materialize with +1 spark for each ally that left play this turn.
image_alt_text: Golden feather with brown shaft and warm orange glow.
justification: Phoenix feathers are the classic shorthand for rising after something else falls. The more allies that left play, the more the next one can come back roaring.

effect_text: Characters you return to hand gain +2 spark.
image_alt_text: Pale yellow parchment pages with dark handwritten notes and tiny diagram.
justification: Something returned to hand feels like a note being taken back up and reread. Repeated handling makes it more charged, so the spark bump is easy to justify.

effect_text: When an ally dissolves an enemy in judgment, it gains +2 spark.
image_alt_text: green book with a coiled serpent embossed on the cover
justification: A knife in judgment reads like the exacting edge of a sentence being carried out. If the ally finishes an enemy that way, it should absolutely come back sharper.

effect_text: Your unblocked attackers score 1 additional point.
image_alt_text: Blue-green fish with orange belly, sharp teeth, and speckled scales.
justification: A piranha is built for slipping past defenses and making a bite count. Unblocked attackers scoring an extra point is the cleanest fish-logic fit here.

effect_text: Your ally with the most spark scores double ✪.
image_alt_text: red fiery eye orb with vertical slit pupil on a brown base
justification: The highest-spark ally is the one everything reflects around, and an orb suggests that central focus. Doubling its score feels like the board mirroring its brilliance.

effect_text: When you dissolve or banish an enemy character, gain 2● and draw a card.
image_alt_text: curved black horn or claw with cream spots and bright green interior
justification: A leech draining life and resources is almost the effect text in object form. If it helps kill or banish something, getting cards and energy back makes sense.

effect_text: Whenever you banish a character, gain 1●.
image_alt_text: Green potion bottle with black skull emblem and toxic glow.
justification: Poison is the obvious icon for stripping enemies away and turning that loss into value. Banishing feels like the cleanest way to squeeze energy out of the toxin.

effect_text: When you abandon an ally, its ▸ Dissolved: ability gains Echo
image_alt_text: dark red sealed envelope tied with ribbon and gold wax seal
justification: A letter can be passed on, copied, or answered later, which is a decent model for Echo. Abandoning an ally and leaving behind a louder version of its death text fits.

effect_text: Allied characters have '▸ Dissolved: Draw a card.'
image_alt_text: Cream-colored rolled parchment scroll with softly shaded edges.
justification: Parchment is where a dissolved ally can leave behind a written afterimage of itself. Drawing a card off the dissolve reads like the page giving up one more line.

effect_text: Once per turn, when an ally is dissolved, return a random character with lower cost from your void to hand.
image_alt_text: golden root vegetable with green leaves and twisted trailing tendrils
justification: A mandragora is a root that looks like it could drag something back from underground. Pulling a lower-cost character from the void is a reasonable plant spell.

effect_text: When an ally is dissolved, materialize a random character with lower cost from your void.
image_alt_text: Gray stone bowl with green leaves and pale golden pestle or stem.
justification: A mortar and pestle suggests grinding one thing down so something smaller can be recovered from it. That makes a recycled void character feel natural.

effect_text: At the end of your turn, materialize a random character you abandoned this turn. It has 1 spark.
image_alt_text: Dark magenta flower petals layered into a velvety blossom.
justification: Petals fall off, scatter, and then somehow show up again when the flower is done. An abandoned ally returning at end of turn fits that loop well enough.

effect_text: When you draw your second card each turn, gain 1 maximum ●.
image_alt_text: golden clam shell with a pale pearl-like interior and striped lid
justification: A shell is a tiny vault that can hold a little more every time it closes. Gaining maximum energy when the second card lands feels like capacity opening up.

effect_text: Your ● is conserved between turns.
image_alt_text: Faceted red-orange crystal with bright translucent highlights.
justification: A philosopher's stone is the classic symbol for carrying value across turns without losing it. Conservation between turns is basically alchemy as bookkeeping.

effect_text: Once per turn, when you play a ↯fast card, draw a card.
image_alt_text: Blue steel wand or dagger with brown wooden handle and glowing tip.
justification: A fast card should feel like a snap-cast wand flick. Drawing another card immediately is exactly the sort of twitchy spellwork a wand implies.

effect_text: When you play your second character each turn, gain 1●.
image_alt_text: golden ornate key with curled filigree bow and bright polished shaft
justification: The second character is the partner that unlocks the rest of the turn, so a second key works cleanly. The image reads like a door opening on more energy.

effect_text: Characters cost you 1● less if one of your characters scored points during that turn.
image_alt_text: Shallow golden bowl holding sparkling violet powder or liquid.
justification: Powder is the sort of thing that makes a reaction go further once momentum already exists. If one ally scored points, cheaper characters as a follow-up feels right.

effect_text: Characters with cost 5● or more cost you 2● less.
image_alt_text: Glass vial of bright purple powder with cork stopper.
justification: A potion is the most direct alchemy image for making costly things easier to handle. Cutting big characters down in price feels like a brewed discount.

effect_text: When you play a card with cost 5● or more, draw a card.
image_alt_text: red-wrapped wand with blue-purple crystal tip and hanging green leaves
justification: A larger card should feel like a deliberate spell with a little more weight to it. The wand image makes the extra draw read as a payoff for casting hard.

effect_text: When you score points during Judgment, draw a card.
image_alt_text: Glossy red lollipop or wand with a tapered silver stick.
justification: A pin is how you mark a result or keep score in place, which suits Judgment well. If judgment points are happening, the pin can turn that into a follow-up draw.

effect_text: At the end of your turn, if you played no cards during your main phase, gain 1✪.
image_alt_text: orange poppy-like flower with dark purple center and soft glowing petals
justification: A maquis flower blooming when you leave the turn quiet gives the effect a nice natural cadence. No cards played becomes the kind of stillness that earns a star.

effect_text: At the end of your turn, if you played no characters this turn, draw a card.
image_alt_text: Black-and-white swirling feather or seed form against blue background.
justification: A rabbit tail is a luck charm, and luck is exactly what you want when you end the turn with nothing in play. The empty board becomes a lucky draw instead.

effect_text: The first time each turn your hand becomes empty, draw a card.
image_alt_text: Black cauldron filled with bubbling neon green liquid.
justification: An empty pot is a container waiting to be refilled, so the first empty-hand trigger makes sense. Once the hand is spent, the pot gives back another card.

effect_text: When 2 or more allies score points, draw a card, then discard a card.
image_alt_text: wooden staff topped with glowing green gem and wrapped root-like branches
justification: A staff can stand in for two allies working together, and blending often ends with one thing being thrown away. Draw then discard feels like a rough magical mix.

effect_text: The first card you buy at each Shop costs 25% less essence and gains a random transfiguration.
image_alt_text: brown book embossed with a paw print medallion on the cover
justification: A charm-studded staff feels like a shop trinket that can bend the first purchase into something better. Extra transfiguration on the first buy reads like a little store magic.

effect_text: The first card you purchase from each Shop is added to your deck twice.
image_alt_text: silver-framed mirror with cracked glass, purple jewel, and curled ornamentation
justification: A mirror is the cleanest object for duplication. Buying the first card twice is exactly what reflection does when you squint at it from the right angle.

effect_text: Each Shop site provides 2 additional options.
image_alt_text: purple-black orb with branching cracks and a bright white glowing center
justification: A shop orb feels like a fortune-teller widening the menu instead of narrowing it. More options at each site is just the ball showing you a few extra futures.

effect_text: Cards you purchase from Shops gain a random tranfiguration.
image_alt_text: Rainbow-capped mushroom with tan speckled stem and golden underside.
justification: The shop keepsake changes color as soon as you buy it, which makes a mushroom a clean symbol for random transfiguration.

effect_text: The first reroll at each Shop costs 0.
image_alt_text: Silver circular emblem with a white shard symbol on lavender and magenta background
justification: A coin is the natural lucky charm for a free reroll, and the witchy emblem sells the idea of bending the shop once.

effect_text: After you buy a card at a Shop, replace it with another card.
image_alt_text: Beige scroll sealed with red ribbon and gold wax medal.
justification: A scroll can be rewritten after the purchase, so the bought card gets replaced the way a revised order gets filed.

effect_text: If your deck has 35 or more cards, cards you add to it are transfigured.
image_alt_text: Blue faceted crystal shard with glowing pale center.
justification: Once the deck is heavy enough, new cards have to pass through a stone threshold and come out altered.

effect_text: When you gain this, apply a Prismatic Transfiguration to each starter card in your deck.
image_alt_text: Translucent aqua shell with rainbow-dotted cap and flowing ruffled skirt.
justification: Prismatic energy fits a rainbow jellyfish well, and starter cards can drift through that sheen just once at the start.

effect_text: Each dreamscape, the first event card you add to your deck gains the Golden Transfiguration.
image_alt_text: Orange-brown star anise pod with eight pointed segments.
justification: A star-shaped spice feels like a gilded signpost, so the first event card can take on a golden version of itself.

effect_text: At Transfiguration sites, when you transfigure an Event, add a copy of it to your deck.
image_alt_text: red spellbook with turquoise gem, gold corners, and green ribbon
justification: A single rune is already a copied mark, so transfiguring an event into a duplicate feels like letting the inscription resonate.

effect_text: When you add a character card to your deck, it has a 50% chance to gain a random transfiguration.
image_alt_text: Pale white mouse with red eyes and pink tail, curled in profile
justification: A lab-white rat is the right kind of test subject for a 50 percent mutation roll on any character card.

effect_text: Once each dreamscape, the first card you add to your deck without a transfiguration gains one.
image_alt_text: Glowing blue crystal facets with white highlights and soft purple shadows
justification: The first plain card picks up a new facet the way a crystal catches light the first time it is touched.

effect_text: Cards you add to your deck from battle rewards gain a random applicable transfiguration.
image_alt_text: Swirling crimson and pink feather or flower petal form.
justification: Battle rewards scatter like petals, and each fallen petal can take on a different transfiguration as it lands.

effect_text: At each Transfiguration site you visit, transfigure two cards instead of one.
image_alt_text: brown strapped book with leaf corners and parchment peeking out
justification: A sack of runed stones looks packed with enough magic to push two cards through the site instead of one.

effect_text: At each Essence site, you are instead offered a Dreamsign.
image_alt_text: Open blue-gray clam shell holding a glowing pearl.
justification: An Essence site offering a Dreamsign feels like an open shell presenting a pearl, not just a pile of energy.

effect_text: Double the essence you gain from essence sites.
image_alt_text: Red star emblem over a white and gray circular badge.
justification: A second ring naturally reads as a doubled payout, so the essence gain cleanly becomes twice what it was.

effect_text: At each Essence site, you may forgo the essence gain to purge 2 cards from your deck.
image_alt_text: Silver crescent sickle with teal glow and wooden handle.
justification: A sickle is a cutting tool, so forgoing the essence to trim two cards feels like a neat harvest.

effect_text: When you Purge a card, gain 50 essence.
image_alt_text: Brown human skull with hollow eye sockets and teeth.
justification: A skull feels like the kind of marker that charges a price for every card you purge, turning each removal into essence.

effect_text: When you enter a new Dreamscape, gain essence equal to 20% of your current essence.
image_alt_text: Brown banded worm curled in an S shape with glossy segmented rings
justification: The worm curls back on itself, which matches carrying a percentage of your current essence into the next Dreamscape.

effect_text: While you have at least 300 essence, Draft picks show 1 additional card.
image_alt_text: Olive-green spiny shell or seed pod dotted with bumps.
justification: The many spines make it feel like an abundance crown, which suits getting an extra draft choice when essence is already high.

effect_text: When you win a battle, gain 10 essence for each of your characters that was dissolved during that battle.
image_alt_text: White and ochre bird skull with elongated beak.
justification: A stripped bird skull feels like battlefield remains, so dissolved characters becoming essence is easy to read off it.

effect_text: When you draft cards, see an additional choice.
image_alt_text: Bright blue feather fan with green leaves at the base
justification: A wing opens the view, and an extra draft choice is basically one more card in the fan.

effect_text: Cards in your deck cannot be purged. Draft sites show 1 additional card.
image_alt_text: Silver segmented shell or armored dome, shaded white and gray.
justification: A tortoise shell says protection and stubbornness, which fits a deck that simply will not be purged.

effect_text: Before each battle, if you have more than 30 cards in your deck, you may purge cards of your choice while remaining at or above 30 cards.
image_alt_text: Copper-brown scorpion with segmented tail and raised claws.
justification: A scorpion clamps down hard, like the pre-battle purge that trims the deck while still respecting the 30-card floor.

effect_text: After you draw your opening hand, you may discard any number of cards, then draw that many.
image_alt_text: Glossy red tongue-like organ with raised bumps and wet highlights
justification: A tongue flicks out, grabs a few cards, and tosses them back, which mirrors discard-then-draw perfectly.

effect_text: When you gain this dreamsign, you may purge up to 4 starter cards from your deck.
image_alt_text: Cream-colored paired teeth with smooth ivory shading and dark root gaps
justification: Pulling starter cards out feels like extracting teeth, and the paired set keeps the loss tidy.

effect_text: When you gain this dreamsign, pick an event card in your deck. That card is always in your opening hand.
image_alt_text: Red spotted goblet filled with yellow liquid and a curled handle
justification: An event card kept in your opening hand is like a cup served first: it is there before anything else has happened.

effect_text: All character cards in your deck are Survivors in addition to their original card types.
image_alt_text: Olive green spotted toad with dark limbs and splayed webbed feet
justification: Toads survive rough ground by default, so turning every character into a Survivor feels surprisingly natural.

effect_text: After each battle, you may purge a card from your deck.
image_alt_text: Pale beige conch shell with scattered brown speckles.
justification: A slug sheds and slimes its way onward, which matches getting one post-battle card out of the deck.

effect_text: After each battle, you may duplicate a card in your deck.
image_alt_text: Black and orange spider with blurred legs and open fangs.
justification: A spider naturally makes copies in a web, so duplicating a card after battle feels like a fresh strand.

effect_text: Instead of receiving Battle Reward cards, choose a card in your deck and duplicate it.
image_alt_text: Translucent green slime mound with rounded gelatinous bulges.
justification: The slime mound can split and bulge into another copy, which suits choosing one card and duplicating it outright.

effect_text: At Duplication sites, you always add 4 copies of the selected card to your deck.
image_alt_text: Cream and orange spotted spiral shell with rounded bands.
justification: A spiral shell naturally implies repetition, so adding four copies at a duplication site feels like a fuller coil.

effect_text: After winning a battle, you are offered a Dreamsign Draft instead of the battle reward pick.
image_alt_text: Glowing red wedge-shaped gem or cheese-like shard with oval cutouts.
justification: A flashy wedge can be traded instead of taken as-is, which makes the battle reward turning into a draft pick believable.

effect_text: Dreamsign Offering and Dreamsign Draft sites are enhanced.
image_alt_text: Green leafy sprig with pale veins and soft brown stem.
justification: Rosemary reads as a blessing herb, so enhancing both Offering and Draft sites feels like the site has been seasoned.

effect_text: In each dreamscape, one additional site is Enhanced.
image_alt_text: Bright red faceted crystal gem with sharp reflective planes.
justification: A bright faceted gem looks like a site that has been polished into a stronger, better-lit version.

effect_text: Your dream journey effects apply twice, if able
image_alt_text: Pale pink segmented worm curled into a hook shape with soft beige shading
justification: The long curled shape gives the sense of an effect stretching out and looping back on itself, which is the heart of double application.

effect_text: When you gain this dreamsign, choose another dreamsign you own. This dreamsign becomes a copy of it.
image_alt_text: Stitched tan voodoo doll with pins, red button eye, and rough fabric
justification: A voodoo doll is literally a stand-in for something else, so copying another dreamsign is its whole trick.

effect_text: Bane cards cannot be added to your deck.
image_alt_text: Off-white skull with dark eye sockets and long beak-like nasal opening.
justification: A raven skull carries the right kind of omen to keep banes out of the deck before they can settle in.

effect_text: When you gain a bane, gain 100 essence.
image_alt_text: Orange spiked egg with yellow spots and ember-like glowing highlights
justification: A strange fruit feels like a cursed bargain you should not take, which makes getting essence from a bane fit the warning.

effect_text: When you gain this dreamsign, add two Nightmare cards to your deck. Cards that cost 4● or more cost 1● less.
image_alt_text: Bound bundle of pale green herbs with purple and yellow flowers
justification: A twisted herb bundle suggests a tainted brew, so adding Nightmares and discounting expensive cards reads like a bad infusion.

effect_text: Draw 2 additional cards during your draw step. At the end of each turn, discard your hand.
image_alt_text: Antler-like bone branch in beige and brown, branching into smooth curves.
justification: Twin horns suggest abundance tipping into excess, which matches drawing more cards than you can comfortably keep and shedding the rest at turn end.
